title: Geometry Dash-Inspired Pixel Art Platformer with Level Editor & Leaderboard

requirements.text: Implement a Motoko backend actor that stores and retrieves all persistent game data: player profiles (principal → username, levels unlocked, attempts per level, best score per level), community-published levels (id, creator principal, title, tile data, play count, ratings), and a global leaderboard (top scores per level across all players).
acceptanceCriteria: Player profiles keyed by Internet Identity principal are stored and retrievable
Best score and attempt count per level per player are saved and updatable
Completed/unlocked level state persists across sessions
Community levels can be created, published, listed, and retrieved by ID
Global leaderboard returns top N entries (player name + score) per level ID
All state survives canister upgrades

requirements.text: Implement Internet Identity login/logout in the frontend. Authenticated players see their username, progress, and created levels. Unauthenticated visitors can browse and play levels but cannot save progress, publish levels, or appear on the leaderboard.
acceptanceCriteria: Login / logout buttons are visible in the navigation
Authenticated state is reflected app-wide via React context
Unauthenticated users can view and play levels
Only authenticated users can submit scores, save progress, or publish levels
Player's principal is used as their identity key in the backend

requirements.text: Build a rhythm-based 2D platformer game engine in the frontend using React and a canvas/animation loop. The player controls a cube character that auto-runs forward; pressing Space or tapping causes the cube to jump. Collision with spikes or obstacles kills the player and restarts the level. The game tracks percentage completion and a score based on how far the player progresses. On level completion, save the best score and increment attempt count via the backend.
acceptanceCriteria: Cube auto-runs and jumps on Space / tap input
Collision detection with spike and block obstacles kills the player
Death restarts the level and increments attempt counter
Completion percentage is displayed as a HUD element during play
Level completion screen shows final score, attempts, and a 'Next Level' button
Best score and attempts are submitted to the backend on completion or death

requirements.text: Design and include at least 3 pre-built levels with increasing difficulty encoded as tile-map data. Each level should have a distinct color theme, obstacle density, and rhythm pattern (timing of obstacles to match a beat feel). Levels are displayed in a level-select screen sorted by difficulty.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 3 pre-built levels exist and are playable
Each level has a unique name, color palette, and increasing obstacle density
Level-select screen shows each level with its name, difficulty indicator, the player's best score, and attempt count
Levels are unlocked sequentially (complete level N to unlock N+1)

requirements.text: Build a full drag-and-drop pixel art tile-grid level editor. The editor canvas shows a scrollable horizontal grid. A sidebar palette contains all tile types: empty, ground block, spike, jump pad, and decorative tiles. Players drag tiles from the palette onto the grid. The editor supports undo/redo, a play-test mode to try the level in-editor, and a Publish button that saves the level to the backend under the authenticated player's account.
acceptanceCriteria: Scrollable horizontal grid canvas is rendered with correct pixel art tile visuals
Tiles can be dragged from palette and placed or erased on the grid
Undo and redo work for tile placement/erasure actions
Play-test mode launches the level in the game engine inside the editor
Authenticated users can publish the level with a title; it appears in the community hub
Unauthenticated users see a prompt to log in when they attempt to publish

requirements.text: Build a Community Levels hub page where players can browse published levels. The page lists levels with their title, creator name, play count, and average rating. Players can click a level to play it and submit a 1–5 star rating after completion. Authenticated players can also delete their own published levels.
acceptanceCriteria: Community hub lists all published levels with title, creator, play count, and average rating
Clicking a level launches it in the game engine
After completing a community level, authenticated players can submit a star rating
Authenticated creators see a delete button on their own levels
Play count increments each time a level is started

requirements.text: Build a Global Leaderboard page that displays the top 10 scores per level (both pre-built and community levels). Each entry shows rank, player name (derived from their principal or a set display name), score, and date achieved. Tabs or a dropdown allow switching between levels.
acceptanceCriteria: Leaderboard page is accessible from the main navigation
Top 10 scores per level are fetched from the backend and displayed
Each row shows rank, player name, score, and date
Level tabs or dropdown allow switching which level's leaderboard is shown
The authenticated player's own entry is highlighted if they appear in the top 10

requirements.text: Apply a cohesive pixel art visual theme throughout the entire application: chunky pixel-style typography (e.g., a pixel/retro Google Font), a dark background with vibrant neon accent colors, pixelated sprites for the cube character and all obstacle/tile types rendered on canvas, and a pixel-bordered UI for all menus, cards, and buttons. The style should feel like a retro arcade game.
acceptanceCriteria: A pixel/retro font is used for all headings and UI labels
The overall color palette uses a dark background with neon accent colors (non-blue/purple)
The cube character and all tile types have distinct pixel art sprites rendered on canvas
Buttons and card components use pixel-style borders and hover effects consistent with a retro arcade aesthetic
The visual theme is consistent across all pages: home, level select, game, editor, community hub, and leaderboard

requirements.text: Render the generated pixel art sprite sheet assets (cube character, obstacle tiles, background tiles, UI icons) as static image files served from frontend/public/assets/generated.
acceptanceCriteria: Sprite sheet images are placed in frontend/public/assets/generated
Game engine and level editor reference these files for tile and character rendering
Assets are visually consistent with the pixel art theme

constraints: All backend logic must live in a single Motoko actor in backend/main.mo
Authentication must use Internet Identity only — no third-party auth providers
Do not edit any files under frontend/src/hooks/ or frontend/src/components/ui
Generated image assets must be placed in frontend/public/assets/generated and not routed through the backend
nonGoals: External database or cloud storage integration

required: Pixel art sprite sheet with cube player character in idle, jumping, and dead states on a transparent background (cube-sprites.dim_256x64.png)
Pixel art tile sheet containing ground block, spike, jump pad, and decorative background tile variants on a transparent background (tile-sprites.dim_512x64.png)
Pixel art parallax scrolling background scene with dark sky, geometric cityscape silhouette, and neon grid ground for level backdrop (level-bg.dim_1920x360.png)
Pixel art game logo for 'Pixel Dash' with neon glow effect and retro styling on a transparent background (logo.dim_512x128.png)
